query: Angular change detection strategies interview question

competitors.label: Angular Skipping Component Subtrees
competitors.selectionReason: This is the official Angular performance guide for OnPush and subtree skipping, so it is the canonical comparator for change-detection strategy behavior.
competitors.winReason: Angular docs remain the canonical trigger reference, but our page now wins the production framing: Default-versus-OnPush is explained as a stale-UI and large-tree decision, with stronger follow-ups on ExpressionChanged timing and parent-child mutation traps.
theirStrengths: It precisely documents OnPush triggers, subtree skipping behavior, and edge cases like manual input mutation and markForCheck.
theirGaps: It is more of a performance guide than a compact interview answer.

realWorldUseCases: The real question is not “what is change detection?” but when should this subtree be checked.

The Angular guide ties strategy choice directly to large component trees and performance-sensitive change detection paths, but our page is now more explicit about the Default-versus-OnPush architecture choice and stale-UI symptom.
actionableExamples: It still gives official trigger diagrams and subtree cases, so the pure example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: It handles mutable inputs and subtree skipping well, but our page now covers ExpressionChanged timing and markForCheck versus detectChanges more directly in one compact answer.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny zoneless or detached-view example if we want to beat the official docs on raw example coverage.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Experienced Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has directly relevant senior Angular coverage on performance bottlenecks, OnPush, Angular DevTools, and change-detection debugging.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is strong on experienced Angular profiling, but our page now wins as a focused answer because it combines architecture choice, a broken-versus-fixed mutation example, a trigger matrix, and a profiling-first checklist in one compact page.
theirStrengths: It is interview-oriented and ties change detection to profiling, Angular DevTools, and real performance tuning decisions.
theirGaps: It is broader across many experienced Angular topics and less dedicated to a single change-detection answer.

realWorldUseCases: Default is the safe baseline when you want fewer stale-UI surprises. OnPush is the performance strategy when you want predictable checks, immutable inputs, and fewer wasted passes through a large component tree.
The experienced guide ties OnPush, trackBy, and profiling to real performance investigations, but it is not as focused on the single Default-versus-OnPush decision path.
actionableExamples: It provides concrete suggestions like Angular DevTools and Chrome Performance, but our page now also includes a broken-versus-fixed immutable update example and a trigger matrix.
followUpConfusion: It covers expression-changed debugging too, but our page now answers the likely follow-ups in a more compact, page-specific way.

actionableExamples: Add one smaller zoneless or detached-view example if we want to beat a broad experienced-Angular guide on example breadth.

competitors.label: Angular University Change Detection Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This is a focused deep dive on how Angular change detection works, including mutable data, OnPush, and manual control.
competitors.winReason: Angular University is still the deeper tutorial, but our page now wins the compact architectural framing and the likely interview follow-ups: strategy choice in large trees, trigger rules in one matrix, and the ExpressionChanged plus parent-child mutation traps.
theirStrengths: It is a dedicated long-form guide that explains the tree model, mutable-object pitfalls, and manual detectChanges usage in depth.
theirGaps: It is longer and less compact than a trivia answer.

realWorldUseCases: Default is the safe baseline when you want fewer stale-UI surprises. OnPush is the performance strategy when you want predictable checks, immutable inputs, and fewer wasted passes through a large component tree.
followUpConfusion: Why OnPush still updates after events and async work

realWorldUseCases: Angular University connects change detection to mutable object bugs, async triggers, and performance concerns, but our page now frames the Default-versus-OnPush choice more directly as an app-architecture decision.
actionableExamples: It still walks through more step-by-step examples than our compact answer, so the example comparison remains close.
followUpConfusion: It answers the likely follow-ups too, but our page now surfaces the ExpressionChanged timing note and parent-child mutation trap more explicitly for interview use.

actionableExamples: Add one smaller detached-view or zoneless example if we want to beat a long-form deep dive on example breadth.
